chemistry.reaction_name: Artemisinin synthesis via photogenerated singlet oxygen oxidation
chemistry.scope_summary: Synthesis of antimalarial drug artemisinin directly from plant extracts of Artemisia annua containing both precursor DHAA and chlorophyll photocatalyst using segmented flow conditions

key_observations_supporting_mechanism: Nonsensitized photochemistry uses mostly UV light with substrate alone excited
EDA complex enables visible light activation with individual substrates not excited
Photoredox catalysis involves SET cycles with molecular photocatalyst
Electronic band catalysis uses non-molecular photocatalyst with band gap excitation
figures.caption: Figure 1. Flow Photochemistry: A Perfect Match. (A) Nonsensitized photochemical transformations; (B) photochemistry via electron donor–acceptor (EDA) complex; (C) concept of photoredox catalysis; (D) photocatalysis by semiconductors and analogs; (E) typical setups for flow photochemistry; (F) attenuation of light irradiance with distance from light source.

figures.flow_path: liquid and gaseous phases in segmented flow configuration
figures.reactor_geometry: tubular with multiple configurations including tube-in-tube, falling-film, and packed-bed
figures.light_source_position: external illumination with LED sources (420 nm, >280 nm UV, white LED, blue LED)
figures.key_information: Multiple photochemical reactor configurations shown: (A) Segmented flow with liquid/gas phases, (B) Tube-in-tube with methylene blue sensitizer, (C) Falling-film with blue LED and TiO2, (D) Packed-bed with porphyrin catalyst and supercritical CO2, (E) Segmented flow suspension with SelectFluor and CMB-C3N4
figures.caption: Figure 2. Multiphasic Systems in Flow Photochemistry. (A) Gas–liquid segmented flow [28,31,33]; (B) tube-in-tube reactor for gas–liquid reactions [38]; (C) falling-film (micro)reactor [41]; (D) packed-bed reactor [47,48]; and (E) segmented flow for solid–liquid reactions [53].

figures.flow_path: inlet to outlet through parallel tubes/capillaries
figures.key_information: Multiple reactor configurations shown: (A) three reactors in series for aglain synthesis, (B) kilogram-scale trifluoromethylation with reaction coil and lamp, (C) segmented flow photochemistry with parallel capillaries, (D) firefly reactor with parallel quartz tubes, (E) laser photoredox in CSTR with fiber optic laser and beam expander

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Limited light penetration and non-uniform irradiation in batch photochemistry
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Better light penetration, more uniform irradiation, shorter reaction times, improved safety, and enhanced scalability
translation_logic.what_changed: Reaction setup changed from batch to continuous flow with segmented flow configuration
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Photocatalyst (chlorophyll), substrate (DHAA), and product (artemisinin) remained the same
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Improved safety due to continuous processing and better heat dissipation

figure_descriptions: Photocatalytic mechanisms comparison showing different activation pathways
Multiple reactor configurations for flow photochemistry including segmented flow, tube-in-tube, falling-film, and packed-bed reactors
Scale-up reactor designs with parallel tube configurations and various light sources

notes: This is a review paper focusing on flow photochemistry concepts rather than a specific reaction study. The artemisinin example is used as an illustrative case but lacks detailed quantitative data. Reactor and light source information derived from figures showing multiple reactor configurations.
